elements.sent-2411-001: We consider a branched transport problem with weakly imposed boundary conditions.
elements.sent-2411-002: This problem arises as a reduced model for pattern formation in type-I superconductors.
elements.sent-2411-003: For this model, it is conjectured that the dimension of the boundary measure is non-integer.
elements.sent-2411-004: We prove this conjecture in a simplified 2D setting, under the (strong) assumption of Ahlfors regularity of the irrigated measure.
elements.sent-2411-005: This work is the first rigorous proof of a singular behaviour for irrigated measures resulting from minimality.
elements.sent-2411-006: Ginzburg-Landau theory \cite{landau predicts two types of superconductors, usually named type-I and type-II.}
elements.sent-2411-007: While in type-II superconductors vortices appear \cite{bbh,sandierserfaty , in type-I superconductors a positive surface energy penalizes the interface between normal and superconducting regions.}
elements.sent-2411-008: Such superconductors, when subject to an applied external magnetic field, exhibit complex patterns \cite{pro1,pro3,pro2 .}
elements.sent-2411-009: This behaviour suggests that branching patterns should appear inside the sample.
elements.sent-2411-010: Results in this direction in the form of scaling laws were first proved for some simplified models in \cite{ChCoKoOt,Choksi2004 and were more recently extended to the full Ginzburg-Landau model in \cite{CoOtSe2016 .}}
elements.sent-2411-011: Besides the anticipated uniform branching regime where, as its name suggests, the magnetization is (approximately) uniform at the boundary of the sample, a surprising finding of \cite{ChCoKoOt,Choksi2004,CoOtSe2016 is the presence of a regime where it is energetically favourable for the magnetic field to be far from uniform at the boundary.}
elements.sent-2411-012: The aim of this paper is to better understand this non-uniform branching regime and more specifically the magnetization pattern at the boundary of the sample.
elements.sent-2411-013: Due to the various lengthscales involved, the full Ginzburg-Landau model is too complicated to analyze, see \cite{CGOS .}
elements.sent-2411-014: We thus make a few simplifications.
elements.sent-2411-015: First, we consider the asymptotic regime of very small external magnetic field in the cross-over between uniform and non-uniform branching regimes.
elements.sent-2411-016: In this regime, formal computations suggest that the normal region collides into a one-dimensional structure.
elements.sent-2411-017: The magnetization, which lives inside the normal region by the Meissner condition, can then be represented as a measure.
elements.sent-2411-018: The reduced model to study should be a branched transport problem inside the sample complemented by a penalization of the deviation from uniformity at the boundary.
elements.sent-2411-019: Because of the magnetic field energy outside the sample, this penalization comes through a Sobolev norm of negative exponent (thus in a weak norm).
elements.sent-2411-020: Let us point out that deep inside the uniform branching regime, a similar model has been rigorously derived in \cite{CGOS .}
elements.sent-2411-021: In that case, the internal energy remains the same but the boundary condition is rather imposed as a hard constraint.
elements.sent-2411-022: The rigorous derivation of the model considered in this paper is work in progress.
elements.sent-2411-023: A second simplification we will make in this paper is to consider a 2D model rather than the original 3D model.
elements.sent-2411-024: Actually, since vertical and horizontal variables play a different role, we rather think of our 2D setting as being a one-dimensional but time dependent branched transport problem.
elements.sent-2411-025: As in \cite{minimizers2d , where a similar 2D model for the uniform branching regime was studied, the simple geometry of $\R$ as opposed to $\R^2$ turns out to be very useful.}

elements.sent-2411-299: If we denote by $\mathcal{H ^\alpha$ the $\alpha$-dimensional Hausdorff measure, the Hausdorff dimension of a set $A \subseteq Q$ is defined as \begin{equation}\label{def:dim_set} \dim A = \sup \{ \alpha \in [0, d] \colon \mathcal{H}^\alpha(A) >0\} = \inf\{ \alpha \in [0, d]\colon \mathcal{H}^\alpha(A) = 0 \}.\end{equation}.}

elements.sent-2411-303: Let us introduce the notion of upper $\alpha$-Ahlfors regular and lower $\alpha$-Ahlfors regular measures for $\alpha \in [0, d]$.
elements.sent-2411-305: A measure is called upper $\alpha$-Ahlfors regular if there exists $M>0$ such that \begin{equation}\label{e:malphareg} \sigma(B_r(x)) \leq M r^\alpha \quad for all x \in \supp\sigma and r\in (0,1].\end{equation}.

elements.sent-2411-306: A measure is called lower $\alpha$-Ahlfors regular if there exists $M>0$ such that \begin{equation}\label{e:malphacon} \sigma(B_r(x)) \geq M r^\alpha \quad for all x \in \supp\sigma and r\in (0,1].\end{equation}.
elements.formula-2411-096: \begin{equation}\label{e:malphacon} \sigma(B_r(x)) \geq M r^\alpha \quad for all x \in \supp\sigma and r\in (0,1].\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-307: If both conditions hold (possibly for different $M,m>0$ respectively), the measure is called $\alpha$-Ahlfors regular.
elements.sent-2411-308: Notice that if $\alpha=d$ upper $\alpha$-Ahlfors regularity is equivalent to $\sigma=f dx$ for $f\in L^\infty$.
elements.sent-2411-309: Notice also that if $\alpha' < \alpha$ then \begin{equation}\sigma is upper \alpha-Ahlfors regular \Rightarrow \sigma is upper \alpha'-Ahlfors regular\end{equation} and \begin{equation}\sigma is lower \alpha'-Ahlfors regular \Rightarrow \sigma is lower \alpha-Ahlfors regular.\end{equation}.
